questions.question: Explain the Two-Phase Commit protocol and its phases.
keyPoints: Two-Phase Commit (2PC) is a distributed transaction protocol
Phase 1 (Prepare): Coordinator asks all participants to prepare to commit
Participants lock resources and vote Yes or No
Phase 2 (Commit): If all vote Yes, coordinator sends Commit; otherwise sends Abort
Participants make changes permanent or rollback

questions.question: What is the blocking problem in Two-Phase Commit? How does Three-Phase Commit address it?
keyPoints: Blocking problem occurs when coordinator fails after Prepare phase
Participants are left waiting indefinitely for Commit or Abort decision
They cannot unilaterally decide to commit or rollback
Three-Phase Commit adds PreCommit phase to avoid blocking
If coordinator fails in PreCommit, participants can safely abort

questions.question: Differentiate between flat and nested distributed transactions.
keyPoints: Flat transactions: Single level, all-or-nothing, simple structure
Nested transactions: Hierarchical with parent and child sub-transactions
Nested allows partial commits - child transactions can commit independently
Nested provides better modularity and fault isolation
In nested, parent coordinates children but failure handling is more complex

questions.question: Explain the role of Transaction Manager and Resource Manager in distributed transactions.
keyPoints: Transaction Manager (Coordinator) orchestrates the entire transaction
It communicates with all participants, manages commit/abort decisions
Resource Manager (RM) manages actual data resources like databases
RMs participate in commit protocol, lock resources, vote Yes/No
RMs execute commit or rollback based on coordinator's decision

questions.question: What is the Saga pattern in distributed transactions? When is it preferred over 2PC?
keyPoints: Saga uses a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions
Each transaction publishes events; if one fails, compensating transactions undo previous work
Preferred over 2PC for: long-running transactions, microservices architecture, loosely coupled services
2PC introduces tight coupling and blocking, unsuitable for distributed microservices
Saga provides better scalability and resilience for loosely coupled systems

questions.question: Explain the CAP theorem and its relevance to distributed transactions.
keyPoints: CAP theorem: Distributed systems can guarantee only two of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
During network partition, must choose between Consistency or Availability
For transaction systems, consistency is often prioritized over availability
CAP theorem influences design choices in distributed databases and transaction systems
No distributed system can guarantee all three properties simultaneously during partition

questions.question: What are the ACID properties and how are they maintained in distributed transactions?
keyPoints: Atomicity: All-or-nothing commit via commit protocols (2PC/3PC)
Consistency: Transaction takes DB from one valid state to another
Isolation: Concurrency control via locking mechanisms
Durability: Logging and recovery mechanisms ensure committed data persists
In distributed systems, these properties are challenging due to multiple resources and network issues

questions.question: Describe the Two-Phase Commit protocol in detail with a diagram. Also explain the coordinator failure scenario and how it affects participants.
keyPoints: Two-Phase Commit has Prepare and Commit phases
Coordinator sends Prepare to all participants, participants vote
If all vote Yes, Coordinator sends Commit; otherwise Abort
Coordinator failure after Prepare causes blocking problem
Participants wait indefinitely - cannot commit or rollback unilaterally
This is a major limitation of 2PC
Solutions include using backup coordinators, timeouts with presumed commit, or using 3PC
Diagram should show message flow between coordinator and participants

questions.question: Compare Flat and Nested distributed transactions. Discuss the advantages of nested transactions with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Flat transactions: Single unit, all operations at same level, all-or-nothing
Nested transactions: Hierarchical structure with parent-child sub-transactions
Nested allows partial commits - child can commit independently
Better modularity and reusability of transaction components
Fault isolation - failure in child doesn't necessarily affect parent
Example: Bank transfer with multiple account operations as children
Compensation handling for partial failures in nested transactions
Overhead of maintaining hierarchy vs simplicity of flat transactions

questions.question: Explain the Three-Phase Commit protocol. How does it overcome the limitations of Two-Phase Commit?
keyPoints: 3PC adds an extra PreCommit phase to eliminate blocking
Phase 1 (CanCommit): Coordinator asks if participants can commit
Phase 2 (PreCommit): If all Yes, coordinator sends PreCommit to prepare for final commit
Phase 3 (DoCommit): Coordinator sends DoCommit for actual commit
If coordinator fails in PreCommit, participants know others prepared - they can safely commit
If coordinator fails before PreCommit, participants can safely abort
Eliminates uncertainty state that causes blocking in 2PC
Still has limitations - does not guarantee in asynchronous systems

questions.question: What are the challenges in implementing distributed transactions? Discuss the solutions used in modern distributed systems.
keyPoints: Challenges: Network failures, coordinator/participant crashes, blocking, latency
2PC blocking problem - solved by 3PC or Saga pattern
Network partitions - CAP theorem trade-offs
Distributed deadlocks - detection and resolution algorithms
Message ordering and duplication handling
Solutions: Saga pattern for microservices, event-driven architecture
Compensating transactions for failure recovery
Use of distributed databases with eventual consistency
Timeout mechanisms and retry logic for reliability
